segments.s035-g1: فریدون is the subject (proper name). چو شد is the temporal clause marker چو 'when' + شد (past of شدن) = 'when he became'. بر جهان کامگار is a prepositional/circumstantial phrase: بر + جهان = 'over/on the world' and کامگار (postpositive adjective) describes his state ('successful/masterful over the world').
segments.s035-g2: ندانست is the negative past (he knew/recognized not). جز خویشتن شهریار is the object/phrase specifying the exclusion: جز = 'except', خویشتن (emphatic reflexive) = 'himself', شهریار = 'king/sovereign' — together meaning 'he recognized no king except himself.'
segments.s035-g3: به رسم کیان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'according to the custom/rite of the Kians' (به + رسم + کیان). تاج و تخت مهی lists two items joined by و ('and'), with مهی (postpositive adjective) qualifying them as 'moonlike/beautiful.'
segments.s035-g4: بیاراست is a past verbal form meaning 'he adorned/arrayed' (poetic past). با کاخ شاهنشهی is an accompanying/instrumental phrase 'with an imperial palace' (با = with; شاهنشهی = 'imperial/royal'). The verb takes that prepositional phrase to describe how/with what he adorned the items.
segments.s035-g5: به‌روز خجسته is a temporal phrase 'on the auspicious day' (به‌روز = 'on the day', خجسته = 'auspicious'). سر مهر‌ماه means 'the head/start of the month Mehr' (i.e., the first day of Mehr); together they form the time expression.
segments.s035-g6: به سر بر نهاد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'he placed upon the head' (به/بر + سر = 'onto the head'; نهاد = past of نهادن 'put/placed'). آن کیانی کلاه is the object 'that Kayanian hat' (آن = that; کیانی qualifies کلاه). Word order places the locative/goal phrase before the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s035-g7: زمانه is the subject 'the age/era'. بی‌اندوه is a compound adjective 'without sorrow' and گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned'. از بدی uses از to indicate cause/reason ('because of/from evil'), so the clause states that the era became sorrowless due to the removal/absence of evil.
segments.s035-g8: گرفتند is past 3rd plural of گرفتن 'they took/seized', here meaning 'they adopted' or 'took up'. هر کس 'every person' follows (poetic word order put the verb first). ره ایزدی is the object 'the divine path/way' that each person took.
segments.s035-g57: This is a poetic list of weapons and armor. همان is a demonstrative ('that/the same') pointing back to previously mentioned garments; جوشن, زوپین, تیغ are nouns listed in coordination with و ('and'). خود here appears in the list as an item (poetic/archaic word for a piece of armor or shield); the sequence is simply noun + و + noun + … with no verb in this segment.
segments.s035-g58: کلاه و کمر continues the inventory (helmet and girdle). هم here functions as an additive adverb ('also/too'), and نبودش is the past negative with the enclitic -ش ('there was not/it was not'). دریغ is used nominally: combined with نبودش it means 'no reluctance / no withholding' — i.e. there was no lack or sparing of these items.
segments.s035-g59: همه qualifies خواسته here as 'all (that was) desired' — خواسته functions substantively ('the requested/desired things'). بر شتر is the prepositional phrase 'onto the camel', and بار کرد is the compound verbal idea 'loaded' (بار 'load' + کرد 'did' as the past-tense verb).
segments.s035-g60: دل‌پاک is an adjectival compound 'pure‑hearted' placed before the verb. سوی is the preposition 'toward', جهاندار is the addressee/title 'sovereign' or 'ruler of the world', and کرد is the simple past 'did' used as the full verb meaning 'directed/turned (his heart/attention)'.
segments.s035-g61: فرستاد is the past tense 'he sent'. نزدیک modifies فرزند ('the near/close son' or 'the son nearby') and functions attributively; چیز is the direct object 'a thing' (i.e. a message or gift). The word order is somewhat topical/poetic (adj + noun before the object).
segments.s035-g62: زبانی 'in speech/words' (or 'a message') is the noun, پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of', آفرین is 'praise / acclamation', and داشت is the past 'had'. نیز adds 'also/too' — altogether: 'it had words full of praise as well.'
segments.s035-g63: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن خواسته is the demonstrative noun phrase 'that request/wish', and دید is the past verb 'saw'. شاه زمین ('king of the land') follows the verb in this poetic order but functions as the grammatical subject.
segments.s035-g64: بپذرفت is the past 'he accepted' (the prefixed ب- is common in literary forms). و is 'and', بر is the preposition 'upon/over', مام is a dialectal/poetic word for 'mother' (here the indirect object of praise), کرد is the light verb 'made/did', and آفرین is 'praise/acclamation' — together 'he accepted and gave praise to the mother.'
segments.s035-g33: نیایش‌کنان is an adverbial/participial form meaning 'praying' (a circumstantial modifier). شد is a past auxiliary here ('became/was'), so نیایش‌کنان شد conveys 'he became/was in prayer' or 'he began praying'. سر و تن are the coordinated direct object ('head and body'), and بشست is a past-tense main verb ('he washed'), placed at the end as is common in Persian (subject omitted, understood 'he').
segments.s035-g34: به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'into the presence of / before'; جهان‌داور is the addressee ('the world-judge', i.e. God). آمد is the past tense 'he came'. نخست is an adverb 'first' placed after the verb for emphasis — overall: 'first he came into the presence of the world-judge.'
segments.s035-g35: نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put'. آن سرش = 'that head of his' (آن = 'that', سر = 'head', ش = enclitic 'his'). پست 'low' modifies the head; بر خاک 'upon the earth'. The repeated بر is a poetic placement of the preposition 'on/upon' for rhythm and emphasis.
segments.s035-g36: همی‌خواند is an imperfective/habitual past form with the prefix همی- indicating continued or repeated action ('kept reciting'). نفرین is the object 'a curse'. به ضحاک بر = 'against Zahhāk' (به + name with a final بر giving the sense 'against/upon'); بر here functions as an adversative/targeting preposition.
segments.s035-g37: همی again marks repeated/continued action ('kept'). آفرین functions as an exclamation/noun 'praise' or 'acclamation' rather than the verb 'to create'. خواند is past 'he uttered/recited'. بر کردگار = 'to/for the Creator' (بر here 'toward/to').
segments.s035-g38: بر here introduces the reason or object ('on/for'). آن 'that' modifies the following noun phrase. شادمان 'joyful' qualifies گردش روزگار, where گردش 'turn/circuit' and روزگار 'fate/time' together mean 'the turn of fate' or 'a change in fortune'. The phrase means 'on that joyful turn of fortune.'
segments.s035-g39: وزان پس is a compound archaic/poetic temporal phrase 'and from that / after that'. کسی را که is a topicalized relative phrase 'anyone who...' (را here marks the topicalized 'کسی'). بودش نیاز is a predicative construction: بود (past copula) + ش (enclitic 'his') and نیاز 'need' — together 'who had need / was in need.'
segments.s035-g40: همی‌داشت is the habitual/imperfective past 'would keep/was keeping' (همی- again marks continued action). روز بد خویش is the noun phrase 'his bad day' (روز 'day', بد 'bad'; خویش is expressed here as خوی + ش — the reflexive/possessive 'his own'). راز is the thing kept: 'secret'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect, but grammatically it says 'he would keep the secret of his bad day.'
segments.s035-g81: همه (before the noun) = “all/everyone”; دست برداشته is a participial/past construction literally “hand(s) lifted” (برداشته is a past participle/perfect used adjectivally or to indicate a simultaneous action). بآسمان is the prepositional phrase with the bound preposition بـ- + آسمان = “to the sky.” Word order is typical Persian: determiner + noun + participle + goal.
segments.s035-g82: همی is an older/poetic adverbial particle marking iterative or continued action (“kept / continually”); خواندندش is past plural third person (خواندند) with the enclitic object -ش = “them/him/it,” so “they called him/they called out to him.” به + نیکی here forms an adverbial phrase “with goodness / in a good spirit” (به used to mark manner); گمان = “expectation / belief.” The clause as a whole reads as a continuing verbal action expressing their hopeful calling.
segments.s035-g83: که introduces the reported/optative clause here (“that / that … may”). جاوید = adjective “eternal.” بادا is an optative/formal subjunctive particle meaning “may it be” (archaic/poetic). چنین شهریار = “such a king” (چنین as demonstrative/adverbial intensifier before the noun). The sequence is a conventional wish formula: که + adjective + بادا + such + noun.
segments.s035-g84: برومند = adjective “prosperous/noble”; بادا again is the optative “may it be”; چنین modifies روزگار (“such times”). The verbless sequence is a parallel wish to the previous clause (elliptical: ‘may such times be prosperous’). Word order: adjective + optative + modifier + noun.
segments.s035-g85: و زآن پس is a temporal sequence: و = “and/then,” زآن = “from that,” پس = “after” (together “and after that”). فریدون is the subject. به گرد جهان = prepositional phrase where به + گرد (here ‘around/about’) + جهان = “around the world”; this marks the direction/extent of his going.
segments.s035-g86: بگردید is an older/poetic past/iterative form of گشتن meaning “he went about / roamed” (the prefix بـ in poetry often marks the imperfective/iterative or is archaising). و دید is simple past “and he saw” (دید = saw); آشکار و نهان are coordinate adjectives “the manifest (visible) and the hidden.” The verbs describe his action of roaming and perceiving both open and concealed things.
segments.s035-g87: هر آن چیز = “every/each thing”; کز (classical contraction of که + از) = “which/from which” (introducing a relative clause); راه بیداد = “the route/way of injustice” where بیداد = “injustice/oppression”; دید = past “he saw.” The whole segment is a relative clause: ‘every thing which, on account of injustice along the road, he saw.’
segments.s035-g88: هر آن بوم و بر = “every land and region” (بوم = native soil/land, بر = surface/upon); کآن here is the classical/poetic relative connector (in context it functions like که آن = “that/which was”) introducing the following predicate نه آباد (not prosperous/inhabited); دید = “he saw.” Note: کآن in the edition’s lemma list also has other lexical senses (mine/source), but here it functions as the relative/locative connector “که آن.”
segments.s035-g65: بزرگان لشگر is a noun phrase 'the great ones (chiefs) of the army' (no ezafe explicitly written, poetic order). چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشناختند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural of شناختن ('they recognized/learned'); the clause means 'when they learned/recognised'.
segments.s035-g66: بر + NP indicates movement/attack 'against/upon' the NP. شهریار جهان is a title-like apposition 'the king/sovereign of the world' (جهان qualifying شهریار). تاختند is past 3pl of تاختن/تازیدن meaning 'they rushed/attacked'.
segments.s035-g67: که‌ای here is an archaic/poetic vocative construction equivalent to 'O' addressing someone; شاه is the addressee 'king'. The adjectives/epithets پیروز (victorious) and یزدان‌شناس (literally 'God‑knower', i.e. pious) follow the noun in the usual Persian order.
segments.s035-g68: This phrase uses nouns and old pronominal/dative forms: ستایش 'praise' is the noun, مر is an archaic form meaning 'to/for me' (a dative/source marker), and او را is the object 'him' with the article/marker را. The second half 'و زویت سپاس' parallels the first: سپاس 'thanks' with زویت an archaic prepositional/pronominal form meaning 'from/for you' (poetic). The whole line expresses giving praise to him (by the speaker) and thanks (to/for you).
segments.s035-g69: چنین روز روزت is an emphatic/poetic piling: 'such a day, your day'. فزون باد is an optative formula where باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)' and فزون is predicative 'more/abundant'; بخت (fortune) is the theme being wished for. The literal structure is 'Such a day — your day — may fortune be abundant.'
segments.s035-g70: بد اندیشگان is a compound meaning 'those who think ill' (ill‑wishers/evil‑thinkers). را marks them as the target of the curse/wish. نگون باد بخت is another optative curse: نگون 'ill‑fated/overturned' with باد 'may', and بخت 'fortune' as the item being turned — 'may the fortune of the ill‑wishers be overturned.'
segments.s035-g71: تو را باد پیروزی is another optative formula: باد is 'may', پیروزی 'victory' is the wished thing, and تو را (you + object marker را) functions as the dative/beneficiary 'to you' in poetic older Persian order. از آسمان is a prepositional phrase 'from heaven' giving the source of the victory.
segments.s035-g72: مبادا is a negative optative meaning 'may it not be' or 'let it not happen'. The verbless clause that follows is elliptical: به جز 'except', داد و نیکی 'justice and goodness' are the only permitted objects of گمان (literally 'thought/conjecture'); thus the sense is 'may there be no thought except justice and goodness.'
segments.s035-g89: This clause means 'he stopped/fettered every evil hand by kindness.' The preposition به + noun (به نیکی) marks the means ('by/with kindness'), followed by the past verb ببست (he bound/closed). از + همه + دست بد ('from every evil hand') is a prepositional phrase indicating what was restrained. Word order here places the instrumental phrase before the verb, and دست بد is a genitive/nominal phrase governed by از.
segments.s035-g90: چنانک introduces a comparison or justification ('as/just as'), followed by از ره هوشیاران where از + ره = 'from/the way' and هوشیاران is the plural adjective/noun 'the sensible/wise ones.' The final سزد is an impersonal present/past-fused copular-like verb meaning 'it befits' or 'it is proper.' Together the clause says that the preceding action accords with the way of the wise.
segments.s035-g91: بیاراست is a past/perfective verb meaning 'he adorned' (verb first here, a poetic inversion). گیتی is the direct object 'the world,' and بسان بهشت is a simile 'like paradise' modifying how he adorned the world. The verb is active and the simile follows the object.
segments.s035-g92: به جای گیا is a prepositional phrase 'in place of plants/vegetation' (به جای = 'instead of / in the place of'); سرو گلبن بکشت is a compound object (cypresses and rosebuds) with بکشت ('he planted/made to grow') as the verb. The verb is archaic/poetic in form but functions as a past action 'he planted.'
segments.s035-g93: از آمل گذر سوی تمیشه کرد is built from two prepositional phrases plus an action: از آمل = 'from Amol,' گذر = 'a passing/passage' (used as a verbal noun), سوی تمیشه = 'toward Tamisheh,' and کرد is the light verb supplying the action ('he made/did' → 'he passed'). The light verb construction (noun + کرد) yields the past action 'he passed.'
segments.s035-g94: نشست اندر آن نامور بیشه کرد is another noun + light-verb or verb + light-verb pattern: نشست ('he sat/settled') with اندر آن نامور بیشه ('in that famed grove') as the locative phrase, and کرد again functioning as an auxiliary/light verb to indicate completed action — together meaning 'he settled/took up residence in that famed grove.'
segments.s035-g95: کجا is the interrogative 'where.' کز here functions as the relative/prepositional 'that/from which' linking to جهان ('the world'). گوش خوانی combines گوش ('ear/attention') with خوانی (present tense of 'read/say/recite') to mean 'you hear/read' or 'one hears/learns' — همی is an old continuous/aspective particle meaning 'ever/always.' Together the clause asks 'Where in the world do you ever hear (of him)?' (a rhetorical question).
segments.s035-g96: جز این = 'except/aside from this'; نیز adds 'also/even'; نامش ('name' + enclitic 'his') is the object; ندانی is the negative present (you do not know) or an impersonal 'one does not know' in classical usage; همی again gives the habitual/continuous sense. The whole clause says 'Except for this, you would not (ever) know his name.'
segments.s035-g41: نهانش نوا کرد و کس را نگفت is a compact clause: نهانش (نهان + -ش) functions adverbially as 'secretly/hiddenly'; نوا کرد is a light verb construction (نوا = 'sound/melody' used verbally) meaning 'uttered/sounded' (past). و links to the following negative verb phrase; کس را نگفت is literally 'told/not-told to anyone' with را marking the object and نگفت the negative past 'did not say/tell'. The sequence shows two related actions: he uttered it secretly and he did not tell anyone.
segments.s035-g42: همان راز او داشت اندر نهفت is a nominal clause describing possession/state: همان راز 'that very secret' is the topic, او داشت uses داشت (past) to mean 'he had/kept', and اندر نهفت is a two‑word prepositional/participial phrase 'within concealed' — نهفت here conveys the idea of being hidden/concealed. Together: 'he had that secret hidden within (him).' Word order is typical Persian (topic — object — subject — verb/phrase).
segments.s035-g43: یکی هفته زین گونه بخشید چیز reads literally 'one week (he) gave such a thing': یکی هفته = 'one week' (duration/period), زین گونه is an older/poetic phrasing meaning 'in this manner/this sort' (زین = 'of this', گونه = 'kind/manner'), and بخشید (past of بخشیدن) means 'gave/bestowed'; چیز is the direct object 'a thing'. Word order places duration/topic before verb, as common in Persian poetry.
segments.s035-g44: چنان شد که درویش نشناخت نیز is a result clause: چنان شد 'it became such' introduces a subordinate clause with که 'that/so that', درویش (the dervish) is the subject of the embedded verb, and نشناخت is the negative past 'did not recognize' (classical/poetic past form). نیز 'also/even' adds emphasis. The structure conveys that the change was so complete the dervish failed to recognize (him) as well.
segments.s035-g45: دگر هفته مر بزم را کرد ساز: دگر هفته = 'another week' or 'the following week'; مر = archaic object 'me' (to/for me); بزم را = 'the feast/banquet' with را marking the definite object; کرد ساز is a compound where کرد (made/did) combines with ساز (literally 'instrument' or 'tune') — here 'prepared the entertainment/music' or 'set the feast in order.' The verb is past, and object order (indirect object before direct object) reflects older poetic style.
segments.s035-g46: مهانی که بودند گردن‌فراز is a relative clause modifying the guests: مهانی (guests) + که (who/that) + بودند (they were — past plural) + گردن‌فراز (an adjective 'proud/lofty'). The clause describes the guests' character and links back to the feast prepared above.
segments.s035-g47: بیاراست چون بوستان خان خویش: بیاراست is a past or perfective verb 'he adorned/beautified'; چون here functions as 'as/like' or 'when', introducing a comparison or temporal phrase; بوستان خان خویش is 'his garden (boastfully phrased as 'the garden of his lordship' — خویش = 'his own'). Note the classical possessive split (خوی + ش) reflecting poetic compaction.
segments.s035-g48: مهان را همه کرد مهمان خویش is straightforward: مهان را (the guests — definite object), همه (all/everyone) + کرد (he made) + مهمان خویش ('his guests' — مهمان = guest, خویش = his own). The verb کرد takes the object phrase to say he made all the visitors into his personal guests.
segments.s035-g9: دل (del) is the patient/theme 'heart' (no explicit possessive in the line; context implies 'their hearts'). از (az) is the preposition 'from/of' placed after the noun as in classical/poetic Persian. داوری‌ها is the plural noun 'judgments'; the clause-final verb بپرداختند is 3rd-person plural past (perfective) and here means that the subjects (they) 'turned' or 'removed' their hearts away from judging — the precise nuance of پرداختن in this poetic context (to turn away/stop applying) is not given in the provided senses, so its sense index is marked -1.
segments.s035-g10: به آیین یکی is a prepositional phrase 'in/according to one rite' (به here marks manner/accord). یکی is used as a numeral/adjective 'one' modifying آیین. جشن نو ساختند: جشن 'festival' + post-nominal adjective نو 'new' (adjectives commonly follow nouns in classical Persian), and ساختند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they made/established' with the (implied) subject 'they'.
segments.s035-g11: نشستند is the verb in 3rd-person plural past meaning 'they sat down' (the provided lemma/senses were for a different root, so none match exactly here and the sense index is -1). فرزانگان 'the sages/learned men' is the subject, and شادکام 'joyful/fortunate' is a predicate/adjectival modifier; the verb-first order (verb — subject — adjective) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s035-g12: گرفتند is 3rd-person plural past 'they took/seized'. هر یک 'each one' marks distribution; ز (poetic variant of از) indicates source/material/possession ('of' or 'from'), so ز یاقوت جام means 'a cup of ruby' (i.e., a ruby cup). جام 'cup' is the object. Word order here is typical: verb then quantified noun phrase.
segments.s035-g13: می‌روشن is an imperfect/present-tense 3rd-person plural form (mi- prefix) meaning 'they shine' or 'they enlighten' — present tense used for vivid descriptive effect. و simply coordinates. چهره شاه نو is a noun phrase 'the face/appearance of the new king' where the possessive/link (ezafe) is implied in poetry and نو 'new' follows the noun as a postpositive adjective.
segments.s035-g14: جهان نو 'the world (is) new' — نو modifies جهان. ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' introducing cause: 'new by/through'. داد is a noun 'justice' (not the verb 'gave'); و links the two causes. سر ماه نو is a compound 'the head/crest of the new moon' (سر used metaphorically for 'head/start'); the whole phrase reads 'the world is renewed by justice and by the new moon's crest.'
segments.s035-g15: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/ordered/said' used for a ruler. تا introduces the subordinate clause indicating purpose/result ('so that' / 'that'), and آتش is the noun 'fire'. افروختند is 3rd-person plural past 'they kindled' (they lit the fires) — the subordinate clause reports the commanded action.
segments.s035-g16: همه 'all' quantifies the following items; عنبر (ambergris) and زعفران (saffron) are listed substances joined by و. سوختند is 3rd-person plural past of سوختن 'they burned' (intransitive here: the substances were burned as incense). The line describes that all the ambergris and saffron were consumed by fire.
segments.s035-g25: This clause is a compressed, somewhat archaic statement with the imperative verb 'دان' (know) near the end. Word order is inverted for emphasis: the negative verb 'نماند' (does not remain/last) starts, followed by the adverb 'چنین' (thus/so), then the imperative 'دان' (know), and finally the topic 'جهان بر کسی' (the world for anyone). 'بر کسی' here means 'for anyone'.
segments.s035-g26: This is a short verbless clause (elliptical common in verse) completing the thought: 'درو' is a contracted locution meaning 'in it' (در + او → دِرو), followed by the noun 'شادکامی' (happiness) and the negative/predictive verbal form 'نیابی' (you will not find / is not found). 'بسی' is an adverb meaning 'much' or 'enough' modifying 'نیابی'.
segments.s035-g27: Literal order: topic 'فرانک' (the Frank) + negation 'نه' + predicate 'آگاه بد زین نهان'. Here 'نه' negates the following predicate. 'آگاه' = 'aware'; the word 'بد' functions as an archaic past auxiliary (= بود 'was') so the sequence means 'was not aware'. 'زین نهان' = 'of this hidden/secret (thing)'.
segments.s035-g28: This is a content clause introduced by 'که' = 'that'. Word-for-word: 'فرزند او' (his son) + 'شاه شد' (became king) + 'بر جهان' (over the world). 'شد' is the past of شدن (became). 'بر' here marks the domain/over which he is king.
segments.s035-g29: Poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from/because of' introduces cause: 'ز ضحاک' = 'because of Zahhâk'. 'شد' is past tense 'became', followed by the compound 'تختِ شاهی' (the throne of kingship) and the predicate adjective 'تهی' (empty).
segments.s035-g30: 'سرآمد' here means 'the foremost' or 'standout'. The sequence links that epithet to the time: 'سرآمدِ برو روزگار مهی' — 'the foremost of that moon-bright (beautiful) age'. The small word 'برو' is functioning here in a frozen poetic locution meaning roughly 'of that' (it is not the usual literal verb 'go'), and 'مهی' is an adjectival epithet 'moon-like/beautiful'.
segments.s035-g31: 'پس' = 'then/thereupon' opens the line. 'آگاهی آمد' = 'information/news came' (a common impersonal phrasing). 'ز فرخ پسر' literally 'from the fortunate son' (ز = from), where 'فرخ' can be a name or an adjective 'fortunate' modifying 'پسر' (son).
segments.s035-g32: The news is reported by the content clause introduced by 'که' = 'that'. 'به مادر' indicates the recipient ('to the mother'). 'فرزند شد تاجور' literally 'the child became tâjūr'; 'شد' is past of شدن. Note: in classical usage 'تاجور' can appear with different senses (here the manuscript gives 'merchant'), but the grammar is simply 'subject + شد + predicate'.
segments.s035-g17: پرستیدن is an infinitive used as a noun (the act of worship) functioning as the subject; مهرگان (the feast of Mehr) follows it as its object/qualification. دین اوست is a copular clause: دین (religion) is the predicative noun and اوست is the enclitic copula meaning “is his.” Word order is [verbal noun + modifier] then [predicate + copula].
segments.s035-g18: Two coordinated nominal predicates are joined by و. تن‌آسانی (a disposition toward bodily ease) and خوردن (the gerund/infinitive ‘eating’) are presented as parallel subjects/activities; آیین اوست is the predicate: آیین (custom/ritual) + اوست (is his). The clause therefore says these habits constitute his practice.
segments.s035-g19: اگر introduces a conditional clause. یادگار is the predicate/noun 'keepsake' and است is the copula. از او marks provenance ('from him'), and ماه مهر ('the moon of مهر') is the thing presented as that keepsake; here ماه is 'moon' qualified by مهر ('love'/'affection'), so the phrase reads 'if the moon of love is a memento from him.'
segments.s035-g20: بکوش is an imperative 'strive' or 'make an effort.' The conjunction و links the next admonition. به رنج is a prepositional phrase 'because of/by hardship'; ایچ is an emphatic negative 'not at all' and attaches to the negative imperative منمای (here an archaic negative imperative meaning 'do not show/reveal'). چهر is the object 'face/appearance.' Together: 'Strive, and do not at all show a face of suffering.'
segments.s035-g21: ورا is the unstressed object/pronominal adverb 'to/for him' (marks the person affected). بد جهان is an adjectival phrase 'that bad/wretched world.' سالیان پانصد is a temporal adverbial 'for five hundred years' placed after the noun phrase, a typical Persian order for duration.
segments.s035-g22: نیفکند is a past-tense negative form (نی‑ prefix + افکند 'he cast/placed') meaning 'did not cast/let stand.' یک روز is 'one day' (here 'not for a single day'), بنیاد بد is 'evil foundation' (noun + adjective). The clause has an emphatic negative meaning: 'not even for one day did an evil foundation remain/stand.'
segments.s035-g23: جهان is the topic 'the world'; چون here functions as 'as/since' introducing the observation. برو بر نماند is a compact verbal phrase: برو (goes/pass away), بر (prepositional particle 'on/away') and نماند (remains/not remain) — together meaning 'the world goes and does not remain.' ای پسر is a direct address 'O son.'
segments.s035-g24: تو نیز 'you also/likewise' begins the admonition. آز (greed) is the object of the negative imperative مپرست (مَپرست = do not worship), with و linking the second prohibition. انده here functions as the noun 'sorrow/grief' (poetic spelling/variant), and مخور is the negative imperative of خوردن used idiomatically 'do not consume/let it consume you' — 'do not be consumed by sorrow.'
segments.s035-g73: و زآن پس is a temporal connective: و = 'and' linking to the previous narrative, زآن + پس = 'after that' (زآن = 'from that', پس = 'after'). جهان‌دیدگان is the plural subject ('those who have seen the world' / people from various places) and سوی شاه is a prepositional phrase (سوی = 'toward', شاه = 'king') marking the direction of their motion. The verb comes later at the end of the line, as is common in Persian.
segments.s035-g74: ز هر گوشه‌ای is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from every corner' (ز = 'from', هر = 'every', گوشه‌ای = 'corner' with the -ای indefinite/partitive ending). برگرفتند راه is the verb phrase: برگرفتند (past, 3rd pl.) plus راه ('the road') — an idiomatic order 'راه برگرفتند' = 'they took the road / set out'. Note the verb's past plural marking -ند.
segments.s035-g75: همه is a distributive/collective subject ('all' / 'everyone'); زر و گوهر are direct objects listed before the verb (classic Persian often places objects before the verb). برآمیختند is past tense, 3rd person plural of برآمیختن (بر- prefix + آمیزن) meaning 'they mixed/pooled (together)'. The compound prefix بر- here emphasizes the coming together.
segments.s035-g76: به marks the direction/recipient ('into/onto'); تاج سپهبد is a noun phrase with سپهبد (spahbed) as the possessor following the noun (typical Persian order: 'crown of the spahbed'). فرو ریختند is a compound verbal phrase: فرو ('down/in', an adverbial/prefixed element) + ریختند (past, 3rd pl. of ریختن 'to pour'), so together 'they poured (down) / poured into'.
segments.s035-g77: همان is a demonstrative/adjectival 'those' or 'that very'; مهتران (plural) means 'chiefs/elders/leaders' (an honorific plural). از همه کشور‌ش is a locative/genitive phrase 'from all his country/realm' where از = 'from', همه = 'all', کشور + -ش is 'country + his' (enclitic -ش = 'his').
segments.s035-g78: بدان is the fusion of به + آن (literally 'with/at that' or 'in that manner'); خرمی is a noun 'mirth / delight' that qualifies their action. صف زده is a compound where صف ('line/row') combines with زده (a participial/compound element from زدن) to mean 'having formed a line' or 'lined up'. بر درش is a locative phrase 'at his door/gate' (بر = 'on/at', در = 'door/gate', -ش = 'his').
segments.s035-g79: ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from God' or 'to/unto God' in sense of invoking the divine (ز here marks source/recipient in poetry). همی‌خواستند is an imperfective/continuous past construction (همی + خواستند) meaning 'they kept asking / were wishing', an archaic/classical continuous aspect marker. آفرین here functions as a noun 'praise / blessing / acclamation' that they request.
segments.s035-g80: بر آن introduces the target of the acclamation ('on/for that'); the following list (تاج و تخت و کلاه و نگین) is a standard enumerative coordinate series ('crown and throne and cap and gem'), each item joined by و. Note that آن points back to the regalia just described.
segments.s035-g49: وزان پس is a poetic compound meaning "from that time/after that"; it functions adverbially before the noun phrase. همه quantifies the following noun گنج, and the participle آراسته follows the noun as a postpositive adjective (typical in Classical Persian), so همه گنج آراسته = "all the adorned treasures."
segments.s035-g50: فراز آوریده is a participial/compound verbal idea: آوریده (past participle of آوردن) with فراز ("on high/aloft") gives the sense "having brought up/raised (to the surface)." نهان خواسته uses نهان as an adjective/adverb "hidden/secretly" and خواسته as an archaic past/perfect form meaning "he desired/asked" (classical usage), so together it means "he desired them to be hidden" or "he wanted them hidden."
segments.s035-g51: همان is the demonstrative "that/the same" modifying گنج‌ها (plural). را is the definite-object marker attaching to the noun phrase. گشادن is the infinitive/gerund "to open," and گرفت is the past form of گرفتن used here as a light verb indicating onset ("took to"/"began to"); the combination گشادن گرفت therefore means "he began to open."
segments.s035-g52: نهاده is the past participle of نهادن "having placed/put" used adverbially. همه رای is the object phrase "all (the) opinions/counsels" (رای here = "opinion"). دادن گرفت repeats the pattern of an infinitive + گرفت, where دادن (to give) + گرفت = "he began to give"; the clause thus reads "having placed (things), he began to give all counsel."
segments.s035-g53: Here گشادن (infinitive "to open") + در گنج (prepositional phrase "in the treasure/vault") is packaged as the object of دید. The noun گاه ("time/occasion") with دید (past "saw/found") yields the idiom گاه دید = "he found an opportunity/occasion." Word order places the main verb دید at the end, typical of Persian narrative past tense.
segments.s035-g54: درم خوار شد is a nominal-predicate clause: درم (a dirham/small coin) + خوار شد "became cheap/worthless" (خوار = "despised/worthless", شد = "became"). چون introduces the subordinate temporal clause "when/as," and پسر شاه دید "he saw the king's son" places the time of the change; the subject is carried over from the narrative context. The verb دید again appears in the past at the end of the clause.
segments.s035-g55: This is a compact noun phrase: همان "that/the same" modifies جامه "robe" and گوهر "jewel." شاهوار is a postpositive adjective meaning "regal/royal." Classical Persian commonly places adjectives after the noun, and coordination with و links the two items (robe and jewel).
segments.s035-g56: Again همان marks "the same," followed by اسپ "steed/horse." تازی is an adjective after the noun meaning "Arabian" (here specifically an Arabian horse). به introduces a prepositional phrase giving manner/attitude ("with"), and زرین (postpositive adjective) modifies عذار to yield "with golden adornment/ornament." Adjective-after-noun order and prepositional به + noun are typical in classical usage.

sentences.s035-s1: When Fereydun became master of the world, he knew no king but himself.
sentences.s035-s2: Following the Kayanian custom, he arrayed the crown and throne in moonlike splendor, with an imperial palace.
sentences.s035-s3: On the auspicious day, the first of Mehr, he set that Kayanian hat upon his head.
sentences.s035-s4: The age became free of sorrow; because evil was gone, every man took the divine path.
sentences.s035-s29: There was no lack of those same arms and armor — the cuirass, shield, spear and sword, helmet and girdle.
sentences.s035-s30: He loaded all the requested things onto the camel, and with a pure heart turned toward the sovereign.
sentences.s035-s31: He sent something to the nearby son; it also had language full of praise.
sentences.s035-s32: When the king of the land saw that request, he accepted it and bestowed praise upon the mother.
sentences.s035-s17: Praying, he washed his head and body; first he came before the Judge of the World.
sentences.s035-s18: He put that head of his low upon the earth, and kept chanting a curse against Zahhāk.
sentences.s035-s19: He repeatedly exclaimed 'praise' to the Creator on account of that joyous turning of fate.
sentences.s035-s20: From then on, whoever was in need would keep the secret of his ill day.
sentences.s035-s41: They all lifted their hands to the sky and continually called him, hoping well.
sentences.s035-s42: ‘May this king be eternal!’ — so they wished; ‘May such times be prosperous!’
sentences.s035-s43: And after that Fereydun went around the world and roamed and saw the manifest and the hidden.
sentences.s035-s44: He saw every thing that had suffered injustice along the way; he saw every land and land‑surface that was not prosperous.
sentences.s035-s33: When the chiefs of the army learned (of it), they rushed against the sovereign of the world.
sentences.s035-s34: O victorious, God‑knowing king — praise to him from me, and thanks from you.
sentences.s035-s35: May such a day—your day—be abundant in fortune; may the ill‑wishers' fortune be overturned.
sentences.s035-s36: May victory be yours from heaven; may there be no thought except for justice and goodness.
sentences.s035-s45: By kindness he bound the evil hand from all; this is fitting according to the way of the wise.
sentences.s035-s46: He adorned the world like paradise; in place of vegetation he planted cypresses and rosebuds.
sentences.s035-s47: He passed from Amol toward Tamisheh; he settled in that famed grove.
sentences.s035-s48: Where in the world have you ever heard of him? Apart from this, you would not know his name either.
sentences.s035-s21: He voiced it secretly and told it to no one; he kept that very secret hidden within himself.
sentences.s035-s22: For one week he gave such a thing; it became so that the dervish did not recognize (him) either.
sentences.s035-s23: Another week he made the feast for me ready (set up music), for guests who were proud.
sentences.s035-s24: He adorned his garden like a lord; he made all the guests his own guests.
sentences.s035-s5: They turned their hearts away from judgments; in a single rite they established a new festival.
sentences.s035-s6: The sages sat down, joyful; each took a ruby cup.
sentences.s035-s7: They shine, and the visage of the new king; the world is renewed by justice and by the new moon's crest.
sentences.s035-s8: He ordered that fires be kindled; all the ambergris and saffron were burned.
sentences.s035-s13: Know this: the world does not remain so for anyone; in it you will not find much happiness.
sentences.s035-s14: The Frank was not aware of this secret — that his son had become king over the world.
sentences.s035-s15: Because of Zahhâk the royal throne became empty; the foremost (one) of that moon-like age emerged.
sentences.s035-s16: Then news came from the fortunate son to the mother, that the child had become a 'tâjūr' (tâjūr here written as تاجور).
sentences.s035-s9: Worshipping Mehregan is his religion; seeking bodily ease and eating is his practice.
sentences.s035-s10: If the moon of love is a keepsake from him, strive — and for the sake of hardship do not at all show a pained face.
sentences.s035-s11: He left him in that wretched world for five hundred years; not for a single day did an evil foundation endure.
sentences.s035-s12: Since the world passes and does not remain, O son, you too do not worship greed and do not be consumed by sorrow.
sentences.s035-s37: And after that, people from every quarter set out toward the king.
sentences.s035-s38: They pooled all their gold and jewels and poured them into the spahbed's crown.
sentences.s035-s39: Those same chiefs from all his realm, in that joy, drew up in a line at his gate.
sentences.s035-s40: They implored God for acclaim/blessing over that crown, throne, cap, and jewel.
sentences.s035-s25: After that, he brought up all the adorned treasures and desired them hidden.
sentences.s035-s26: He began to open those same treasures; having placed (things), he began to give all counsel.
sentences.s035-s27: He found an occasion to open the treasure; when he saw the king's son, the dirham became of little account.
sentences.s035-s28: The same regal robe and jewel; the same Arabian steed with golden adornment.
